Venezuela's Rodriguez is Aligned With US, Wright Says
data.description:
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright says Venezuela's acting president Delcy Rodriguez is happy to have America's help in rebuilding the country's oil sector. He says dozens of companies are looking to invest and oil production could rise by 40% in the first year. He speaks to Bloomberg's Annmarie Hordern from Caracas.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

Utilities Up After PG&E Earnings - Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers rose amid strong earnings.

PG&E rose after the California utility raised the lower end of its projected earnings range, reflecting strong power demand from data centers.

The report echoed other strong profit updates from Duke Energy and other rivals.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1726ET

data.title: Communications Services Down on AI Fears - Communications Services Roundup
data.description:
Communications-services fell as traders fled sectors subject to disruption by artificial intelligence.

McGraw Hill shares rallied after the educational publisher said use of artificial-intelligence tools for digital offerings boosted earnings growth in the latest quarter.

McGraw was among the specialty publishers hard hit recently when Anthropic launched a tool that conducted analysis of legal archives, stoking fears that lawyers and other professionals would cease paying for expensive journals.

Other specialty publishers, such as Thomson Reuters and News Corp., publisher of this newswire, continued to sell off Thursday.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1726ET

data.title: Palo Alto plans dual listing in Tel Aviv after closing $25 billion CyberArk deal
Tech Down Amid Fears About Tech Boom Sustainability - Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies fell amid renewed fears about the sustainability of a broad tech boom.

The Federal Trade Commission issued a letter to Apple Chief Executive Tim Cook, warning that the company's Apple News product may violate federal law due to its alleged promotion of left-wing publications. Apple shares fell sharply.

Shares of Cisco tumbled after the maker of network technology warned that rising prices of memory chips would squeeze profit margins.

One strategist said the tech sector and the stock market in general has experienced a "bifurcation" recently, as artificial intelligence enters a disruptive phase. "We're seeing an acceleration in creative destruction -- most notably in software," said Lorenzo Di Mattia, manager of hedge fund Sibilla Global Fund.

Anthropic, which makes some of the tools that have caused selloffs for software companies because of the competitive threat, is now valued at about $380 billion as it prepares to raise another $30 billion in capital, The Wall Street Journal reported.

Real-estate Web sites continued a recent slide due to fears of AI competition and a weak home-sales report. Zillow Group and Opendoor Technologies fell by 4% or more.

Porch Group shares rallied after the provider of homeowners' insurance and related software forecast robust 2026 revenue growth.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1726ET

data.title: FedEx expects holiday quarter adjusted profit to top Wall Street's view
AU Small Finance Bank says RBI approves reappointment of Sanjay Agarwal as MD & CEO
Financials Down as Private Credit Fears Offset Deal Activity - Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions fell as fears about housing and private-credit markets offset deal activity.

Nuveen agreed to buy U.K. asset manager Schroders for $13.5 billion, bolstering its business in Europe with a storied London investment house that dates back more than two centuries.

Among private-credit firms, KKR and Blue Owl resumed a selloff after recent strength.

Upheaval in the software industry caused by artificial-intelligence advances "may also pressure private credit funds with concentrated exposure to software borrowers (in many cases over 30% of assets under management)," said Lorenzo Di Mattia, manager of hedge fund Sibilla Global Fund. Di Mattia is currently betting against regional banks.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1724ET

data.title: Consumer Cos Down After Used-Home Sales Data - Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies slid amid housing-market fears.

Used-home sales fell 8.4% in January, the biggest monthly decline since February 2022, after snowstorms and low consumer confidence slowed a housing market that was showing signs of recovery.

Unilever, producer of staples such as Dove soap and Hellman's mayonnaise, said underlying sales growth picked up in the fourth quarter, with an acceleration in Asia offsetting slower demand in the U.S. and Europe.

Other staples firms were not as sanguine. Shares of pest-control firm Rollins, which operates extermination companies such as Orkin worldwide, fell after its fourth-quarter earnings growth lagged Wall Street estimates.

Hyatt Hotels' fourth-quarter loss narrowed and the hotelier forecast a swing to a profit in 2026, amid growth in key performance measures in 2026.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1724ET

data.title: Health Care Down on Defensive Demand - Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies were more or less flat as traders sought out defensive sectors in the wake of weak housing-market data.

Seres Therapeutics plunged after the biotech company warned it was laying off about 30% of its employees and pausing development of a treatment for infections associated with certain stem-cell transplants.

Vertex Therapeutics rose ahead of the drug maker's earnings report.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1723ET

data.title: Industrials Down Amid Fears About AI Impact on Logistics Firms - Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies declined amid fears about the implications of artificial-intelligence advances for freight brokers.

Shares of CH Robinson Worldwide, Expeditors International of Washington and RXO plunged, amid fears that AI tools could automate the shipment coordination and supply-chain management that represents their core business.

Shares of trucking lines such as Saia, XPO and Old Dominion Freight Lines fell sharply.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1722ET

data.title: Arista Networks forecasts upbeat revenue as AI speeding lifts demand, shares jump
data.description:
Feb 12 (Reuters) - Networking equipment provider Arista Networks forecast annual revenue above Wall Street estimates on Thursday, betting on steady demand for its electronic hardware amid the expanding presence of data centers, sending its shares up more than 17% in extended trading.

Demand for Arista's ethernet network switches and routers has surged as companies increase their investment in AI infrastructure.

Analysts have noted that the company has a strong exposure to major cloud and AI customers, whose capex is expected to keep growing sharply, creating a bigger demand backdrop.

Arista Networks makes the high-speed computer networking equipment that powers big data centers, serving cloud and AI titans such as Microsoft and Meta, along with enterprise, financial services and specialized cloud provider customers.

The company forecast first-quarter revenue of about $2.6 billion, above the analysts' average estimate of $2.45 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG.

It reported a fourth-quarter revenue of $2.49 billion, compared with estimates of $2.38 billion. Adjusted profit came in at 82 cents per share, beating estimates of 76 cents per share.

(Reporting by Kritika Lamba and Anhata Rooprai in Bengaluru; Editing by Alan Barona)

data.title: Materials Down With Metals Prices - Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals, grains and other raw materials fell alongside metals prices.

Gold and silver futures plunged ahead of inflation data. Futures of both started 2026 with a torrid rally but have recently seen wild swings. Gold futures fell 2.9% to $4923 an ounce. Silver slid 9.8% to $75.55 an ounce.

South32, an Australian metals producer building a new mine in Arizona, doesn't expect to supply critical minerals to the Trump administration's proposed $12 billion stockpile because the zinc, silver and copper it produces likely would not be included in the program, its chief executive officer said.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-12-26 1722ET

data.title: Energy Down on Growth Fears - Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies fell after a weak housing report stirred economic growth fears.

Oil futures ticked down but remained near multimonth highs as a confrontation between Iran and the U.S. remains unresolved.

The Trump administration covertly sent thousands of Starlink terminals into Iran after the regime's brutal crackdown on demonstrations last month, U.S. officials said, an effort to keep dissidents online.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires
